name: Castor Extended
description: Castor, in Greek mythology, was one of the Dioscuri, twin brothers celebrated for their exceptional horsemanship and athletic prowess. He was the mortal son of Queen Leda of Sparta and King Tyndareus, while his twin Pollux was the divine son of Zeus. Castor's skills with horses and chariots were unparalleled, making him a celebrated hero among warriors and athletes. He participated in numerous heroic adventures, including the Calydonian Boar hunt and the voyage of the Argonauts. Castor's loyalty and deep bond with Pollux are central to his legend, showcasing the ideals of fraternal devotion and courage. Their story highlights the complex relationship between mortals and gods, and the enduring power of brotherly love. Though mortal, Castor's legacy lives on through the constellation Gemini, a testament to his and Pollux's eternal bond. He is remembered as a skilled warrior, a loyal brother, and a symbol of human excellence. The story of Castor and Pollux also explores themes of mortality and immortality, and the lengths to which love and sacrifice can extend, even to the point of sharing divinity. His death and the subsequent intervention of Zeus to allow him and Pollux to share immortality solidified his place in Greek myth as a figure of tragic heroism and divine favor. Castor represents mortal skill and bravery, forever intertwined with the divine grace of his brother.
subtitle: Mortal Twin, Horseman, Brother

keyMyths.title: The Birth of the Dioscuri
keyMyths.description: Leda, Queen of Sparta, laid two eggs, one bearing Castor and Clytemnestra (fathered by Tyndareus) and the other bearing Pollux and Helen (fathered by Zeus). This divine and mortal parentage sets the stage for their intertwined destinies.

keyMyths.title: The Fight with Idas and Lynceus
keyMyths.description: Castor and Pollux fought Idas and Lynceus over cattle and the betrothal of the Leucippides. Castor was killed by Idas, leading to Pollux's grief and Zeus's intervention to grant them shared immortality.

keyMyths.title: Immortality Shared
keyMyths.description: After Castor's death, Pollux, being immortal, begged Zeus to allow him to share his immortality with his brother. Zeus granted their request, allowing them to spend alternate days in Olympus and Hades, symbolizing their eternal bond.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: Mentions the Dioscuri and their shared immortality.
sources.text: Mentions the Dioscuri and their shared immortality.

sources.author: Hyginus
